title: Deployment Environments, OIDC, Workload Identity, and Cloud Authority · Release Evidence Brief: Ci Github Oidc Claims Permission
summary: A deployment uses a long-lived service-account key in repository secrets, trusts any branch subject, grants project-wide admin, bypasses environment review, and leaves credentials reusable after the job. Planned artifact: Add and verify the ci github oidc claims permission increment in environment and federated identity policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Request id-token write only in the deployment job and validate issuer, audience, subject, workflow ref, repository identity, event, and environment claims.
Use named environments for approvals, branch and tag policies, scoped secrets, wait rules, URL evidence, and deployment history.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# cicd-github-actions cumulative delivery-system portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic workflow, policy, artifact, and release evidence review only.
# They never call GitHub APIs, start runners, execute learner commands, access Docker or registries, exchange OIDC tokens, or contact cloud services.

version-baseline:

  node: 24-lts
  typescript-native: 7.0.2
  typescript-api-compatibility: 6.0.2
  build-push-action: 7
  google-auth: 3
  deploy-cloudrun: 3
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-delivery-environment

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example deployment stores a long-lived cloud key, requests broad id-token and repository permissions, and trusts ambiguous OIDC claims. Constrain environment, audience, subject, workflow, revision, service account, approval, and audit evidence. Competency probe: Request id-token write only in the deployment job and validate issuer, audience, subject, workflow ref, repository identity, event, and environment claims. A defensible solution applies Ci Github Oidc Claims Permission, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to environment and federated identity policy.

content.code:
# Worked focus: ci-github-oidc-claims-permission — Request id-token write only in the deployment job and validate issuer, audience, subject, workflow ref, repository identity, event, and environment claims.
  # Evidence: ci-worked-cd-environments-oidc-cloud-auth-4
  - competency: ci-worked-cd-environments-oidc-cloud-auth-4
    artifact: federated-deployment.yaml
    decision: worked cd environments oidc cloud auth 4
    environment: GitHub-hosted ubuntu-24.04 runner on a cold cache
    fault-injection: expire the only retained test artifact during incident reconstruction
    retained-proof: accessible redacted timeline with owner expiry and regression control
    case-key: worked4
    change: |
      environment: production
      reviewers: operations-and-security
      branch-policy: protected-main
      permissions: contents-read-id-token-write
      oidc-issuer: token.actions.githubusercontent.com
      oidc-audience: google-wif-provider
      subject: immutable-repository-workflow-environment
      google-auth-action: 3
      service-account: cloud-run-deployer
      roles: resource-scoped
      credential-lifetime: job-bounded
    verify: inspect environment history OIDC claims provider condition service-account impersonation IAM audit and credential cleanup
    failure: Reject long-lived keys, repository-name-only trust, global admin, approval-free environments, or short lifetime as sufficient control.
    repair: Constrain environment and claims, federate identity, separate service accounts, scope roles, audit exchange, expire credentials, and rehearse revocation.
content.caption: Runnable CI/CD workflow, artifact, release, and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.
